g.data-narration: Welcome to Optimal Binary Search Trees - a powerful technique for minimizing expected search cost
g.text: Dynamic Programming for Minimum Expected Search Cost

g.data-narration: A standard BST structure depends on insertion order, but when we know search probabilities, we can do better
g.text: What is an Optimal Binary Search Tree?
An OBST is a BST structure that minimizes the
expected search cost based on known probabilities of searching for each key.

g.data-narration: In our example, placing key 20 at root gives expected cost 1.30 versus 1.90 - OBST matters!
We have two types of probabilities: p_i for successful searches of key k_i, and q_i for unsuccessful searches
The cost formula counts comparisons needed: each key at depth d needs d+1 comparisons, weighted by its probability
g.text: Expected Search Cost = Σ pᵢ × (depth(kᵢ) + 1) + Σ qᵢ × (depth(dᵢ) + 1)
Note: +1 because node at depth d requires (d+1) comparisons

g.data-narration: OBST has optimal substructure: if root is k_r, left and right subtrees must also be optimal BSTs
g.text: If OBST has root kᵣ, then:

g.data-narration: The weight table w[i][j] stores the sum of all probabilities in a subtree - a key building block
g.text: w[i][j] = Σ pₘ (m=i+1 to j) + Σ qₘ (m=i to j)

g.data-narration: The core DP recurrence: c[i][j] equals weight plus minimum of costs with different root choices
g.text: for r = i+1 to j (all possible roots)

g.data-narration: Let's work through an example with 4 keys and their probabilities to see the DP in action
g.text: n = 4 keys: k₁=10, k₂=20, k₃=30, k₄=40

g.data-narration: First, we initialize the base cases: c[i][i] and w[i][i] both equal q_i for diagonal elements
g.text: For i = 0 to n: c[i][i] = qᵢ and w[i][i] = qᵢ

g.data-narration: For subtrees of length 1 (single key), we try each key as root and compute the cost
g.text: For subproblems (i, i+1) - single key kᵢ₊₁:

g.data-narration: For longer chains, we try all possible roots and use the one that minimizes total cost
The root table stores which key should be at the root of each optimal subtree - use it to build the tree
g.text: Full tables give us root[0][4] = 2 (key 20 at root)

g.data-narration: The basic algorithm is O(n³) time but Knuth's optimization reduces it to O(n²) by restricting root search range
Key takeaways: OBST minimizes expected cost using DP, has optimal substructure, and benefits from Knuth optimization
